notes.anchor: Chrysippus Vettius, freedman of Cyrus the architect
notes.note: A freedman acting as message-bearer; he took his former master's nomen (Vettius) on manumission and is named for the architect Cyrus, a builder Cicero knew. Such freedmen routinely carried verbal greetings between gentlemen and their distant friends.

notes.anchor: the fewer suitors will be losing their cases now with you as their counsel
notes.note: A jurist's joke at Trebatius's expense: if he has forgotten how to write, he has presumably forgotten his law too, so his luckless clients (litigants seeking his counsel) will at least lose fewer cases through his advice.

notes.anchor: the fear of the summer campaigns
notes.note: Aestiva, the summer's military operations of Caesar's army in Gaul; Cicero ribs the unwarlike Trebatius for dodging them, as he had earlier wriggled out of the British expedition.

notes.anchor: the law of friendship by heart, rather than the law of litigation
notes.note: A studied pun on iura: the 'rights/law' (iura) of benevolentia (good-will, friendship) versus those of lites (lawsuits). Cicero wishes the professional jurist had mastered the duties of friendship as thoroughly as the law of the courts.
